source: .specs/agent-scenarios.md → Feature: Multiple sessions in same cwd
notes: Two concurrent pi REPL sessions launched in the SAME cwd via the interactive driver's start_session step (gained after the driver was extended). Each pi process mints its OWN session UUID and writes a distinct transcript under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl (session_id_assignment:false), so the two distinct session_ids are asserted via new_session/same_session_as relations, not literal ids. PHASE-ANCHORING NOTE: every phase that pins identity to a UUID-keyed rollout anchors on a WORKING transition (never a `ready`): pi's process scanner (ExactName{pi}, pgrep -x) emits a transient proc-<PID> presession row born in ready BEFORE the transcript is discovered, so a ready-anchored new_session phase greedily binds to that placeholder and the chained same_session_as pins fail. Anchoring on working skips both the proc-<PID> placeholder AND pi's lazy-materialization first-turn arc (which sometimes batches user+assistant into the initial transcript write within one poll cycle, producing a microsecond-fast working→ready that's not a useful identity anchor), binding identity to a real subsequent turn. The recipe sends TWO prompts per session before start_session/re-drive so a robust observable working arc exists for each session regardless of how pi batches turn 1. PID-BINDING CAVEAT: pi's DiscoverPID = DiscoverPIDByCWD (cwd-only narrowing + a highest-PID disambiguate fallback) — UNLIKE codex's per-rollout-file-writer binding and claudecode's metadata-first lookup; two pi processes sharing one cwd both resolve to the same candidate set, so the daemon CANNOT cleanly bind each same-cwd session to its OWN PID purely by cwd-only narrowing. In practice the algorithm's sticky-once-bound behavior plus highest-PID fallback DOES give the right answer when sessions launch sequentially (the second launch's highest PID is genuinely the new one), but the underlying algorithm remains structurally vulnerable to concurrent launches and PID reuse. This spec deliberately does NOT assert stable per-PID binding — it asserts what the daemon CAN observe correctly: two distinct session_id rows, each progressing through its own working→ready arc on its own UUID transcript, with session 1 staying observable as a distinct id when re-driven after session 2 is alive.

text: Session 1's first daemon-observable working arc (turn 1 if pi emits it, else turn 2 if turn 1 was batched into the lazy transcript write) — binds session 1's UUID-keyed transcript id, dodging pi's transient proc-<PID> placeholder which is born in ready and never enters working
Session 1 turn completes: working → ready on the same UUID transcript

Session 2's first daemon-observable working arc appears in the SAME cwd as a DISTINCT transcript id — new_session proves it is not session 1; anchoring on working skips both session 2's transient proc-<PID> placeholder and any lazy-batched turn 1 arc
Session 2 turn completes: working → ready on session 2's UUID transcript
Session 1 is driven AGAIN while session 2 is still alive — a working transition on session 1's ORIGINAL UUID proves the row stayed observable as a distinct identity, independent of session 2
Session 1's re-drive completes: working → ready on the same UUID, never flapping over to session 2's id
